original_abstract: Abstract This study examines the relationship between critical thinking and grades at the tertiary level, focusing on their social, political, and ethical implications. Employing a mixed-methods approach, this study combines survey data collection with in-depth interviews to generate comprehensive insights into the complex relationship between critical thinking and grades. The survey targets 173 faculty members, while the interviews focus on seven selected academic staff members from Palestinian universities, enabling a comprehensive understanding of the research objectives. Results showed that grades often hinder critical thinking skills and creativity, leading to rote memorization and limited creativity. The study also highlights the political implications of grades, as standardized testing influences education policies and curriculum decisions. Faculty members expressed criticism of the prioritization of grades, citing conventional evaluation methods, temporal limitations, and resource constraints. As critical thinking is crucial for comprehensive student development, contributing to problem-solving, decision-making, creativity, innovation, effective communication, and active citizenship, the study proposes diverse approaches to strike a balance between valuing grades and nurturing critical thinking abilities. By fostering critical thinking abilities, Palestinian students can enhance their preparedness for academic pursuits, personal growth, and societal contributions.
humanized_abstract: Title: The relationship between critical thinking skills and grades and their social, political and ethical implications The study investigated the relationship between critical thinking skills and grades and their social, political and ethical implications. Data was collected using both quantitative and qualitative methods. A survey was carried out on the 173 faculty members at one of the Palestinian universities, in addition to in-depth interviews conducted with 7 faculty members in three Palestinian universities. The results revealed that grades impeded students critical thinking skills as well as their creativity. They were forced to memorize and could not express their ideas in an innovating manner. Grades have political implications as well. The study found that the tests grades have a bearing on the educational policies and the methods of curriculum design. The faculty members criticized the hegemony of the grades due to several reasons such as the conventional evaluation systems, the lack of time for students, the insufficient materials provided to students. The critical thinking skills are one of the important skills which enhance the comprehensive development of students in addition to enabling them to face the various challenges they encounter in life such as making decisions, solving problems, being creative, innovating, communicating effectively and participating in civic activities. Based on the findings of the study, the researcher recommended various methods to maintain the values of grades in the minds of students, as well as to enhance their critical thinking skills. The study suggested that if critical thinking skills were fostered in the students in Palestine, it would lead to their being better prepared academically, socially and morally to face their future challenges whether in the academic, social or professional sectors.
